_meta.description: Per-material troubleshooting: common print failures, root causes, and specific fixes. Agent-consumable for real-time print diagnostics.

common_issues.root_cause: PLA has low viscosity when melted, allowing thin threads to draw between travel moves, especially at higher nozzle temperatures.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature by 5-10C (try 195-200C)
Increase retraction distance to 5-6mm at 40-45mm/s for Bowden, 1-2mm at 30mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.03-0.05mm3 in slicer
Increase travel speed to 150-200mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Store filament in a sealed container with desiccant. Print a temperature tower to find your optimal temp.

common_issues.root_cause: First layer squish combined with heat from the bed causes PLA to bulge outward at the base, creating a wider-than-expected footprint.

fixes.action: Reduce bed temperature to 50-55C after the first 3 layers
Raise Z-offset by 0.02-0.05mm to reduce first layer squish
Enable elephant's foot compensation of 0.1-0.2mm in slicer
Reduce first layer flow rate to 95%

common_issues.prevention: Calibrate Z-offset with a single-layer test print. Use 55C bed temp instead of 60C.

common_issues.root_cause: PLA has a low glass transition temperature (~60C). Heat traveling up the heatbreak softens filament above the melt zone, causing jams in the cold end.

fixes.action: Ensure hotend fan is running at 100% and not obstructed
Lower nozzle temperature by 5-10C to reduce heat conducted upward
Reduce retraction distance to 2mm max for direct drive to avoid pulling molten PLA into the cold zone
Add a 0.5-1 second retraction extra prime amount of 0.02mm3 to compensate

common_issues.prevention: Keep hotend cooling fan clean and unobstructed. Avoid printing PLA in ambient temps above 30C.

common_issues.root_cause: Insufficient nozzle temperature or excessive part cooling prevents layers from bonding properly.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10C (try 210-215C)
Reduce part cooling fan to 50-70% for first 10 layers
Increase line width to 110-120% of nozzle diameter for more contact area
Reduce layer height to 75% of nozzle diameter max (0.3mm for 0.4mm nozzle)

common_issues.prevention: Run a temperature tower to find the sweet spot between adhesion and stringing.

common_issues.root_cause: Moisture-contaminated PLA or degraded filament results in weak inter-layer bonds and micro-voids that concentrate stress.

fixes.action: Dry filament at 45C for 4-6 hours before printing
Increase nozzle temperature by 5C to improve flow and bonding
Increase infill to 25-40% with grid or gyroid pattern
Increase wall count to 4-5 perimeters for structural prints

common_issues.prevention: Store filament sealed with desiccant. Replace filament older than 12 months if stored improperly.

common_issues.root_cause: Differential cooling across large flat surfaces causes internal stresses that pull corners upward, even with PLA's relatively low shrinkage rate.

fixes.action: Apply glue stick or hairspray to build plate for additional adhesion
Increase bed temperature to 60C and keep room draft-free
Add a brim of 8-10mm width in slicer
Reduce part cooling fan to 60-80% for first 15 layers

common_issues.prevention: Use a PEI or textured build surface. Orient large flat surfaces away from the bed when possible.

common_issues.root_cause: PLA sags under gravity on steep overhangs when part cooling is insufficient or print speed is too fast.

fixes.action: Increase part cooling fan to 100% for overhangs beyond 45 degrees
Reduce overhang print speed to 15-20mm/s
Lower nozzle temperature by 5C to solidify faster
Reduce layer height to 0.12-0.16mm for better overhang quality

common_issues.prevention: Orient model to minimize unsupported overhangs. Use supports for angles beyond 60 degrees.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or resealable vacuum bag

break_in_tips: Start with a nozzle temperature of 200-210C and bed at 55-60C. Most PLAs print well in this range.
Level your bed carefully — PLA is forgiving but a good first layer matters. Aim for slight squish with no gaps.
Use 100% part cooling fan after the first 2-3 layers. PLA loves cooling.
Print a temperature tower (190-220C in 5C steps) to find the best temp for your specific brand.
Start at 50mm/s print speed and increase from there once you're getting good results.

common_issues.root_cause: PLA+ additives slightly increase melt fluidity compared to standard PLA, causing thin threads on travel moves.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature by 5C (try 200-205C)
Increase retraction distance to 5-6mm at 40mm/s for Bowden, 1.5-2mm at 30mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.03-0.05mm3
Increase travel speed to 150-200mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Dial in retraction with a retraction tower test. Keep filament dry.

common_issues.root_cause: First layer compression combined with bed heat causes the base to bulge outward. PLA+ is slightly softer than PLA when warm.

fixes.action: Reduce bed temperature to 50-55C after the first 3 layers
Raise Z-offset by 0.02-0.05mm
Enable elephant's foot compensation of 0.1-0.2mm in slicer
Reduce first layer flow to 95%

common_issues.prevention: Calibrate Z-offset with a first-layer test print. Use 55C bed temp.

common_issues.root_cause: PLA+ has a similar glass transition to PLA (~58-62C). Heat traveling up the heatbreak softens filament prematurely.

fixes.action: Ensure hotend cooling fan runs at 100% and check for dust buildup
Lower nozzle temperature by 5-10C
Reduce retraction to 1.5mm max for direct drive
Increase minimum layer time to 10-15 seconds to avoid heat buildup in small parts

common_issues.prevention: Clean hotend fan regularly. Avoid prolonged pauses mid-print.

common_issues.root_cause: Pressure buildup in the nozzle at seam points and travel start positions causes small blobs of excess material.

fixes.action: Set seam position to sharpest corner or rear in slicer
Enable linear advance/pressure advance at K=0.04-0.06 for direct drive
Reduce retraction prime speed to 20-25mm/s
Enable wipe distance of 2-3mm in slicer

common_issues.prevention: Tune pressure advance/linear advance for your specific extruder.

common_issues.root_cause: PLA+ is slightly more flexible than PLA, causing overhangs to sag more before cooling.

fixes.action: Increase part cooling fan to 100% for overhangs
Reduce overhang speed to 15-20mm/s
Lower nozzle temperature by 5C
Reduce layer height to 0.12-0.16mm for steep overhangs

common_issues.prevention: Use supports for overhangs beyond 55-60 degrees.

common_issues.root_cause: Differential cooling across large surfaces creates internal stresses that curl edges upward.

fixes.action: Add a brim of 8-10mm width
Increase bed temperature to 60C
Apply glue stick to build surface
Reduce part cooling to 70% for the first 10 layers

common_issues.prevention: Print in a draft-free environment. Use PEI build surface.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or resealable vacuum bag

break_in_tips: PLA+ prints 5-10C hotter than standard PLA. Start at 205-215C nozzle, 55-60C bed.
PLA+ is tougher and less brittle than standard PLA — great for functional parts that need some flex.
Part cooling at 100% works well. PLA+ responds to cooling similarly to PLA.
Expect slightly better layer adhesion than PLA — you can push speed slightly higher (60mm/s) once dialed in.
If you have PLA settings already, increase nozzle temp by 5C and you're likely good to go.

common_issues.root_cause: Silk additives (mineral powders, waxes) significantly reduce viscosity and increase flow, making stringing much worse than standard PLA.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 195-200C (10-15C below typical PLA)
Increase retraction distance to 6-7mm at 45mm/s for Bowden, 2-3mm at 35mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.06-0.08mm3
Increase travel speed to 180-200mm/s
Enable wipe distance of 3-5mm at nozzle travel moves

common_issues.prevention: Print a retraction tower specifically for silk PLA. Keep nozzle temp as low as possible while maintaining adhesion.

common_issues.root_cause: The silk additives reduce the structural integrity of the filament, causing it to sag more on unsupported overhangs before cooling.

fixes.action: Increase part cooling fan to 100%
Reduce overhang print speed to 12-15mm/s
Lower nozzle temperature to 195C
Use supports for any overhang beyond 45 degrees

common_issues.prevention: Design parts to minimize overhangs when using silk PLA. Accept that overhang quality will be lower than standard PLA.

common_issues.symptom: Weak parts that break easily under load
common_issues.root_cause: Silk additives reduce inter-layer bond strength by 20-40% compared to standard PLA, making prints more fragile.

fixes.action: Increase wall count to 5-6 perimeters
Increase infill to 30-50% with gyroid or cubic pattern
Increase nozzle temperature to 210-215C for better layer bonding
Reduce print speed to 35-40mm/s for better layer adhesion

common_issues.prevention: Silk PLA is cosmetic, not structural. Use standard PLA or PLA+ for load-bearing parts.

common_issues.symptom: Inconsistent shiny finish with matte patches
common_issues.root_cause: Temperature variations across the print cause the silk effect to appear differently. Fan speed changes also alter the surface finish.

fixes.action: Set a consistent fan speed of 60-80% throughout the print
Maintain nozzle temperature at exactly 205C with no fluctuation
Print at a consistent speed of 40-50mm/s without acceleration-induced changes

common_issues.prevention: Consistent temperature and fan speed are key. Avoid rapid speed or fan changes mid-print.

common_issues.symptom: Elephant's foot more pronounced than standard PLA
common_issues.root_cause: Silk additives make PLA softer at bed temperatures, causing more base deformation under the weight of upper layers.

fixes.action: Reduce bed temperature to 50C
Raise Z-offset by 0.03-0.06mm
Enable elephant's foot compensation of 0.15-0.25mm

common_issues.prevention: Use lower bed temperature from the start. Calibrate Z-offset specifically for silk PLA.

common_issues.root_cause: Low viscosity of silk PLA causes more oozing at retraction and seam points, leaving visible blobs that disrupt the silk finish.

fixes.action: Set seam position to rear or inside corner
Enable wipe distance of 3-5mm
Reduce retraction prime extra amount by 0.02-0.04mm3
Enable coasting at 0.05-0.07mm3

common_issues.prevention: Seam placement is critical for silk PLA. Align seams to least-visible surfaces.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or resealable vacuum bag

break_in_tips: Silk PLA is about aesthetics, not strength. Use it for display pieces, vases, and decorative items.
Start at 200-210C nozzle, 50-55C bed. The silk effect looks best at moderate temperatures.
Expect more stringing than standard PLA — budget extra time for post-processing or dial retraction carefully.
Print slower than normal PLA (40-50mm/s) for the best surface finish.
The silk effect is most visible on curved surfaces and large flat areas. Test with a simple vase first.

common_issues.symptom: Nozzle clogging from wood fiber buildup
common_issues.root_cause: Wood fibers accumulate and char inside the nozzle, especially at temperatures above 210C or when filament sits molten for too long.

fixes.action: Use a 0.5mm or 0.6mm nozzle — never use 0.4mm or smaller
Lower nozzle temperature to 190-200C to prevent fiber charring
Perform cold pulls with standard PLA between wood PLA sessions to clear buildup
Reduce retraction distance to 3-4mm to prevent fiber compaction in the cold zone

common_issues.prevention: Never leave wood PLA sitting hot in the nozzle. Unload immediately after printing. Use 0.5mm+ nozzle exclusively.

common_issues.root_cause: Wood fiber distribution varies along the filament, and fibers can partially block the nozzle opening, causing intermittent under-extrusion.

fixes.action: Use a 0.6mm nozzle to reduce blockage frequency
Increase extrusion multiplier/flow to 105-110%
Reduce print speed to 30-40mm/s for consistent flow
Increase nozzle temperature by 5C if under-extruding (but stay below 210C)

common_issues.prevention: Accept some surface variation — it adds to the wood aesthetic. Use larger nozzles for best results.

common_issues.root_cause: Wood PLA has lower viscosity than standard PLA due to the fiber additives disrupting the polymer matrix.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 190-195C
Increase retraction to 5-6mm at 35mm/s for Bowden, 2mm at 25mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.04-0.06mm3
Increase travel speed to 150mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Keep nozzle temperature as low as possible while maintaining flow.

common_issues.root_cause: Wood fibers are mildly abrasive and gradually enlarge the nozzle bore of brass nozzles, degrading print quality over extended use.

fixes.action: Switch to a hardened steel or ruby-tipped nozzle (0.5mm+)
Check nozzle diameter every 50-100 hours of wood PLA printing
Replace brass nozzle after 100+ hours of wood filament use

common_issues.prevention: Invest in a hardened steel nozzle before starting with wood PLA. The cost saves multiple brass nozzle replacements.

common_issues.root_cause: Wood fibers interrupt the polymer chain bonding between layers, reducing adhesion strength compared to pure PLA.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature to 200-205C (but not above 210C)
Reduce part cooling fan to 50-70%
Increase line width to 120-130% of nozzle diameter
Reduce layer height to 0.2mm max for better inter-layer bonding

common_issues.prevention: Wood PLA is for decorative prints, not structural parts. Design for aesthetics, not load bearing.

common_issues.root_cause: Wood fibers char at temperatures above 210-215C, leaving dark streaks and inconsistent color in the print.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 190-200C
Reduce print time per layer to avoid prolonged heat exposure
Purge filament if a clog caused extended residence time at temperature

common_issues.prevention: Never exceed 210C with wood PLA. The wood-grain color variation comes from temperature — slight changes are intentional, charring is not.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant. Wood fibers absorb moisture faster than pure PLA.

break_in_tips: Use a 0.5mm or 0.6mm nozzle — this is non-negotiable. A 0.4mm WILL clog. Never use 0.25mm.
Start at 190-200C nozzle, 50-55C bed. Higher temps cause charring, not better prints.
Print at 30-40mm/s for consistent extrusion. Wood PLA does not like speed.
Temperature changes create wood-grain effects — some slicers can vary temp per layer for a realistic look.
Sand and stain finished prints just like real wood. The fiber content takes stain beautifully.

common_issues.root_cause: PETG is naturally stringy due to its high viscosity and tendency to remain fluid over a wider temperature range than PLA.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 225-230C (minimum that maintains adhesion)
Increase retraction distance to 5-7mm at 40mm/s for Bowden, 2-4mm at 25mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.06-0.10mm3
Increase travel speed to 180-200mm/s
Enable wipe distance of 3-5mm

common_issues.prevention: PETG will always string more than PLA. Accept minor stringing and remove with a heat gun at 200C for 2-3 seconds per area.

common_issues.symptom: Bed adhesion too strong — tears build surface on removal
common_issues.root_cause: PETG bonds chemically to glass and some PEI surfaces at bed temperatures, creating adhesion so strong it damages the surface.

fixes.action: Apply a thin layer of glue stick as a release agent before printing
Increase Z-offset by 0.02-0.05mm to reduce first layer squish
Reduce bed temperature to 70C (from typical 80C)
Let the bed cool completely to room temperature before removal

common_issues.prevention: Always use glue stick on glass/PEI beds. PETG will permanently bond to bare glass.

common_issues.symptom: Blobs and zits on print surface
common_issues.root_cause: PETG oozes at retraction points due to its high viscosity and slow solidification, leaving excess material at seams and travel start positions.

fixes.action: Enable linear advance/pressure advance at K=0.05-0.08 for direct drive
Set seam position to sharpest corner in slicer
Enable wipe distance of 3-5mm and coasting of 0.05-0.08mm3
Reduce retraction prime speed to 15-20mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Tune pressure advance carefully for PETG. It needs higher K values than PLA.

common_issues.symptom: Moisture-related surface defects (bubbling, rough surface, popping sounds)
common_issues.root_cause: PETG readily absorbs atmospheric moisture. Water trapped in the filament vaporizes at the nozzle, creating steam bubbles that disrupt extrusion.

fixes.action: Dry filament at 65C for 4-6 hours
Print from a dry box with inline desiccant
Listen for popping/crackling sounds — if present, filament is wet

common_issues.prevention: Store PETG sealed with desiccant. Dry before every use if stored in open air for more than a week.

common_issues.root_cause: PETG remains fluid longer than PLA, causing unsupported spans to droop significantly before cooling enough to hold shape.

fixes.action: Increase part cooling fan to 80-100% during bridges
Reduce bridge flow rate to 80-90%
Increase bridge speed to 25-30mm/s to stretch the filament thinner
Lower nozzle temperature by 5-10C for bridge layers

common_issues.prevention: Design bridges under 40mm when possible. Use supports for longer spans.

common_issues.root_cause: PETG curls when cooled too quickly on overhangs, causing the nozzle to catch on curled edges and dislodge layers.

fixes.action: Reduce part cooling fan to 50% on overhangs (PETG needs less cooling than PLA)
Lower nozzle temperature by 5C to reduce curling tendency
Reduce overhang speed to 20-25mm/s
Enable Z-hop of 0.2-0.4mm during travel to avoid catching curled edges

common_issues.prevention: PETG overhangs work best with moderate cooling (40-60% fan), not maximum.

common_issues.symptom: First layer adhesion problems on textured PEI
common_issues.root_cause: PETG sometimes does not stick well to textured PEI at lower bed temperatures or with incorrect Z-offset.

fixes.action: Increase bed temperature to 80-85C
Lower Z-offset by 0.02-0.04mm for more first layer squish
Reduce first layer speed to 15-20mm/s
Clean bed with isopropyl alcohol (90%+) and avoid touching the surface

common_issues.prevention: Clean bed before every PETG print. Oils from skin prevent adhesion.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or dry box. PETG absorbs moisture within days of open-air exposure.

break_in_tips: Start at 230-240C nozzle, 75-80C bed. PETG needs higher temps than PLA.
Always use glue stick on glass or smooth PEI beds — PETG will bond permanently otherwise.
Expect stringing. It's normal for PETG. A heat gun at 200C cleans strings in seconds.
Reduce part cooling to 50-70% — PETG does not like maximum cooling like PLA does.
PETG is food-safe after printing (when using food-safe colorant). Great for kitchen items and containers.

common_issues.symptom: Rapid nozzle wear from carbon fibers
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers are highly abrasive and will enlarge a brass nozzle bore within hours of printing, degrading quality rapidly.

fixes.action: Use a hardened steel, ruby, or tungsten carbide nozzle exclusively
Check nozzle diameter with a drill bit gauge every 20 hours of CF printing
Never use a brass nozzle — even a single print can damage it

common_issues.prevention: Invest in a hardened steel nozzle (0.4mm+) before your first CF PETG print.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fiber strands are 50-100 microns and can bridge across nozzle openings below 0.4mm, causing partial or full blockage.

fixes.action: Use a 0.4mm nozzle minimum — 0.5-0.6mm preferred
Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10C to improve flow around fibers
Perform cold pulls with standard PETG to clear partial clogs
Reduce retraction distance to 2-3mm to avoid fiber compaction in heatbreak

common_issues.prevention: Never use a nozzle smaller than 0.4mm. 0.5mm is the sweet spot for CF filaments.

common_issues.symptom: Weaker layer adhesion than plain PETG
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers interrupt polymer chain bonding between layers, reducing inter-layer strength by 15-30% compared to unfilled PETG.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10C above standard PETG (240-250C)
Reduce part cooling fan to 30-50%
Increase line width to 120-140% of nozzle diameter
Reduce print speed to 30-40mm/s for better bonding time

common_issues.prevention: CF PETG is strong in-plane but weak between layers. Orient parts so load is along the XY plane, not Z.

common_issues.root_cause: Inherits PETG's stringing tendency, made slightly worse by fibers increasing effective viscosity at some points and reducing it at others.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to minimum that maintains adhesion (235-240C)
Increase retraction to 5-6mm at 35mm/s for Bowden, 2-3mm at 25mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.06-0.08mm3
Increase travel speed to 150-180mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Dial retraction specifically for CF PETG — settings differ from plain PETG.

common_issues.symptom: Rough matte surface with visible fiber texture
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers protrude slightly from the surface of each layer, creating a naturally rough matte finish that differs from smooth PETG.

fixes.action: Reduce layer height to 0.12-0.16mm for smoother appearance
Increase flow rate by 2-3% to better encapsulate fibers
Print at slower speeds (30-40mm/s) for more consistent fiber embedding

common_issues.prevention: The matte finish is inherent to CF filaments. Sand with 400-800 grit for a smoother look.

common_issues.symptom: Moisture absorption causing rough surface and popping
common_issues.root_cause: CF PETG absorbs moisture slightly faster than plain PETG due to fibers creating micro-channels for water infiltration.

fixes.action: Dry filament at 65C for 4-6 hours before printing
Print from a dry box to prevent moisture re-absorption during long prints
Listen for popping sounds — any crackling means wet filament

common_issues.prevention: Store sealed with desiccant. Dry before every print session if ambient humidity exceeds 40%.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or dry box

break_in_tips: Hardened steel nozzle is mandatory. Brass will be destroyed within a single spool.
Start at 240-250C nozzle, 75-80C bed. CF PETG likes slightly higher temps than plain PETG.
Use 0.5mm nozzle minimum for reliable extrusion. 0.6mm is ideal.
CF PETG is excellent for stiff, lightweight parts. Think drone frames, brackets, enclosures.
Expect a matte, slightly rough finish. This is normal and gives parts a professional carbon-fiber look.

common_issues.symptom: Rapid nozzle wear from carbon fibers
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers are highly abrasive and will destroy a brass nozzle within hours, enlarging the bore and degrading dimensional accuracy.

fixes.action: Use a hardened steel, ruby, or tungsten carbide nozzle exclusively
Inspect nozzle diameter every 20-30 hours of CF printing
Never use a brass nozzle with any carbon fiber filament

common_issues.prevention: A hardened nozzle is mandatory before printing any carbon fiber filament.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fiber strands bridge across small nozzle openings, causing partial or complete blockage that stops extrusion.

fixes.action: Use a 0.5mm nozzle minimum — 0.6mm preferred for reliability
Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10C to improve flow around fibers
Perform cold pulls with standard PETG to clear partial clogs
Reduce retraction distance to 2-3mm to prevent fiber compaction in heatbreak

common_issues.prevention: Never attempt to print CF PETG through a nozzle smaller than 0.4mm.

common_issues.symptom: Reduced flexibility compared to standard PETG
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers increase stiffness dramatically but reduce the ductility and flexibility that standard PETG offers, making parts more prone to brittle failure under impact.

fixes.action: Increase wall count to 5-6 perimeters for impact-prone areas
Use gyroid infill at 25-35% for better energy absorption
Orient parts so impact loads are distributed across the XY plane

common_issues.prevention: CF PETG is for stiffness, not flexibility. Use standard PETG for parts needing impact resistance or flex.

common_issues.root_cause: Inherits PETG's tendency to string, with carbon fibers slightly altering flow dynamics but not reducing oozing.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 235-240C
Increase retraction to 5-6mm at 35mm/s for Bowden, 2-3mm at 25mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.06-0.08mm3
Increase travel speed to 150-180mm/s

common_issues.prevention: PETG-based filaments always string. Post-process with a heat gun at 200C.

common_issues.symptom: Weaker inter-layer adhesion than plain PETG
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers interrupt polymer chain bonding between layers, reducing Z-axis strength by 15-30% compared to unfilled PETG.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature to 240-250C for better fusion
Reduce part cooling fan to 30-50%
Increase line width to 120-140% of nozzle diameter
Reduce print speed to 30-40mm/s for better bonding

common_issues.prevention: Orient parts so primary loads are in-plane (XY), not cross-layer (Z).

common_issues.symptom: Moisture absorption causing surface defects and popping
common_issues.root_cause: PETG base absorbs atmospheric moisture, and carbon fibers create micro-channels that accelerate water absorption throughout the filament.

fixes.action: Dry filament at 65C for 4-6 hours before printing
Print from a dry box for prints longer than 2 hours
Listen for popping or crackling sounds — indicates wet filament

common_issues.prevention: Store sealed with desiccant. Dry before every print session if ambient humidity exceeds 40%.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or dry box

break_in_tips: Hardened steel nozzle is mandatory. Brass nozzles will be destroyed within a single spool of CF PETG.
Start at 240-250C nozzle, 75-80C bed. Slightly higher than standard PETG settings.
Use 0.5mm nozzle minimum for reliable extrusion. 0.6mm is ideal for CF filaments.
CF PETG offers great stiffness with easier printing than CF nylon. Ideal for structural brackets and enclosures.
Part cooling at 30-50%. Less than standard PETG for better layer bonding.

common_issues.symptom: Warping and corners lifting from bed
common_issues.root_cause: ABS has high thermal shrinkage (~0.8%). As layers cool, they contract and pull corners upward. This is ABS's signature failure mode.

fixes.action: Use an enclosure to maintain ambient temperature of 40-50C
Increase bed temperature to 100-110C
Apply ABS slurry (ABS dissolved in acetone) or glue stick to the bed
Add a brim of 10-15mm width
Disable part cooling fan entirely or keep at 0-10%

common_issues.prevention: An enclosure is virtually mandatory for ABS. Even a cardboard box helps. Eliminate all drafts.

common_issues.root_cause: Rapid cooling between layers causes thermal stress that exceeds the inter-layer bond strength, resulting in visible cracks between layers.

fixes.action: Ensure enclosure is sealed and maintaining 40-50C ambient
Increase nozzle temperature to 240-250C for better layer bonding
Reduce part cooling fan to 0% — ABS should not be actively cooled
Reduce print speed to 40-50mm/s for better heat transfer between layers

common_issues.prevention: Never print ABS without an enclosure. Layer cracking is almost entirely a temperature management problem.

common_issues.symptom: Strong fumes and chemical odor during printing
common_issues.root_cause: ABS releases styrene vapors when heated, which are irritating and potentially harmful with prolonged exposure in enclosed spaces.

fixes.action: Use a printer enclosure with an activated carbon filter
Ventilate the room — run an exhaust fan or open windows
Lower nozzle temperature to minimum effective range (230-240C)
Consider switching to ASA for outdoor parts (lower fumes, UV resistant)

common_issues.prevention: Always print ABS in a ventilated area or with filtration. Never print in a bedroom or small unventilated space.

common_issues.root_cause: High bed temperature (100C+) combined with first-layer squish causes ABS to deform at the base, spreading wider than designed.

fixes.action: Enable elephant's foot compensation of 0.2-0.3mm in slicer
Raise Z-offset by 0.03-0.06mm
Reduce first layer flow to 93-95%
Reduce bed temperature to 95C (balance adhesion vs. deformation)

common_issues.prevention: Calibrate first layer height specifically for ABS at your bed temperature.

common_issues.symptom: Corner lifting and curling on tall prints
common_issues.root_cause: Cumulative thermal contraction over many layers creates enough force to peel the print from the bed, starting at corners and edges.

fixes.action: Seal enclosure to maintain consistent 45-55C ambient
Apply ABS slurry (dissolve ABS scraps in acetone) to bed surface
Add mouse-ear pads (small discs) at corners in the model
Increase brim to 15-20mm for tall prints over 100mm
Reduce bed temp difference — use 95C bed with 50C enclosure

common_issues.prevention: For tall prints, ABS slurry + enclosure + brim is the reliable combination.

common_issues.symptom: Surface quality issues — rough or blistered surface
common_issues.root_cause: Moisture in ABS filament vaporizes at the nozzle, creating micro-bubbles that erupt on the surface as rough patches or blisters.

fixes.action: Dry filament at 80C for 4-6 hours
Print from dry box for long prints
Check for popping sounds during extrusion — indicates moisture

common_issues.prevention: ABS absorbs moisture moderately. Dry before printing if left in open air for more than 3-5 days.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant

break_in_tips: An enclosure is not optional for ABS. Even a large cardboard box over the printer reduces warping dramatically.
Start at 240-250C nozzle, 100-110C bed. ABS needs high temperatures everywhere.
Turn the part cooling fan OFF. ABS hates cooling — it's the opposite of PLA.
Apply ABS slurry (ABS dissolved in acetone) to the bed for the best adhesion. Glue stick is the next best option.
ABS can be vapor-smoothed with acetone for a glossy, injection-molded finish. Research the technique before attempting.

common_issues.root_cause: ASA has similar thermal shrinkage to ABS (~0.7%), though slightly less. Without an enclosure, corners will still lift.

fixes.action: Use an enclosure to maintain 40-50C ambient temperature
Increase bed temperature to 95-105C
Add a brim of 8-12mm width
Apply glue stick or ASA slurry to bed surface
Disable part cooling fan or keep at 0-10%

common_issues.prevention: Enclosure is strongly recommended for ASA, though warping is less severe than ABS.

common_issues.root_cause: Temperature differential between layers causes stress that can exceed the inter-layer bond. Less severe than ABS but still present.

fixes.action: Ensure enclosure is at 40-50C ambient
Increase nozzle temperature to 245-255C
Reduce print speed to 40-50mm/s for better bonding
Reduce or eliminate part cooling fan

common_issues.prevention: Keep ambient temps consistent. Avoid opening the enclosure mid-print.

common_issues.root_cause: ASA has moderate viscosity and remains fluid over a wide temperature range, producing strings similar to ABS.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 235-240C
Increase retraction to 5-6mm at 40mm/s for Bowden, 2-3mm at 30mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.05-0.07mm3
Increase travel speed to 150-180mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Print a retraction tower to find the sweet spot for your specific ASA brand.

common_issues.root_cause: ASA emits styrene-based fumes similar to ABS, though generally slightly less intense. Still requires ventilation.

fixes.action: Use a printer enclosure with activated carbon filtration
Ventilate the room during and after printing
Keep nozzle temperature at lowest effective range (235-245C)

common_issues.prevention: Print in a ventilated space. An enclosure with a carbon filter is ideal.

common_issues.symptom: Poor bed adhesion on smooth surfaces
common_issues.root_cause: ASA can struggle to adhere to smooth PEI or glass beds without an adhesion aid, especially at lower bed temperatures.

fixes.action: Increase bed temperature to 100-105C
Apply glue stick or ASA/ABS slurry to the bed
Reduce first layer speed to 15-20mm/s
Increase first layer line width to 150% of nozzle diameter

common_issues.prevention: Glue stick on a heated bed at 100C provides reliable adhesion.

common_issues.symptom: Surface discoloration from UV exposure during outdoor use
common_issues.root_cause: While ASA is UV-resistant, prolonged multi-year exposure can cause slight yellowing or color shift in lighter colors.

fixes.action: Use darker ASA colors (black, gray) for best long-term UV stability
Apply UV-protective clear coat for light-colored parts

common_issues.prevention: ASA is significantly more UV-stable than ABS. Light colors may shift slightly after 2-3 years of direct sun.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant

break_in_tips: ASA is ABS's outdoor-friendly cousin. Use it for anything that sees sun, rain, or heat.
Start at 240-250C nozzle, 95-105C bed. Settings are nearly identical to ABS.
An enclosure reduces warping significantly, though ASA warps less than ABS.
Part cooling fan should be at 0-10% — like ABS, ASA dislikes active cooling.
ASA is the go-to material for outdoor enclosures, garden fixtures, and automotive trim.

common_issues.root_cause: ASA+ has improved warping resistance over standard ASA through additive modification, but still exhibits some thermal contraction on large parts.

fixes.action: Use an enclosure at 40-45C ambient
Increase bed temperature to 95-100C
Add a brim of 6-10mm width
Apply glue stick to bed

common_issues.prevention: Enclosure recommended for prints wider than 100mm. Smaller parts may print without one.

common_issues.root_cause: Similar viscosity profile to standard ASA, producing strings on travel moves.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 235-240C
Increase retraction to 5-6mm at 40mm/s for Bowden, 2-3mm at 30mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.05-0.07mm3
Increase travel speed to 150-180mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Dial retraction settings specifically for ASA+ — they differ slightly from standard ASA.

common_issues.symptom: Layer adhesion failure on tall prints
common_issues.root_cause: While improved over standard ASA, temperature differentials on tall prints can still cause inter-layer bond failure without controlled ambient temperature.

fixes.action: Use enclosure at 40-50C ambient
Increase nozzle temperature to 245-250C
Reduce print speed to 40-50mm/s
Disable part cooling fan

common_issues.prevention: Keep enclosure closed throughout the print. Avoid temperature shocks.

common_issues.root_cause: Like standard ASA, ASA+ emits styrene-based vapors during printing that require adequate ventilation.

fixes.action: Use enclosure with carbon filter
Ventilate the room
Keep nozzle temp at lowest effective range (235-245C)

common_issues.prevention: Always print in a ventilated space or with filtration.

common_issues.root_cause: High bed temperature causes the soft first layer to spread under the weight of subsequent layers.

fixes.action: Enable elephant's foot compensation of 0.15-0.25mm
Raise Z-offset by 0.03-0.05mm
Reduce first layer flow to 95%

common_issues.prevention: Calibrate first layer specifically at your ASA+ bed temperature.

common_issues.root_cause: Moisture absorption or insufficient top layers can cause uneven top surfaces. ASA+ additives can also cause slight surface texture differences.

fixes.action: Increase top layers to 5-6 (or 1.2mm minimum top thickness)
Dry filament at 80C for 4 hours if surface is bubbly
Enable ironing at 10% flow, 200mm/s for smooth top surfaces

common_issues.prevention: Keep filament dry and use adequate top layers.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant

break_in_tips: ASA+ is an improved ASA with better layer adhesion and reduced warping. Same use case — outdoor and UV-exposed parts.
Start at 240-250C nozzle, 95-100C bed. Settings are almost identical to standard ASA.
You may be able to print smaller parts without an enclosure, but keep one handy for anything over 100mm.
Part cooling fan at 0-10%. Same thermal management as ABS and ASA.
If you've dialed in ASA or ABS settings already, use them as your starting point for ASA+.

common_issues.root_cause: TPU is inherently elastic and has very low viscosity when melted, causing severe stringing that is difficult to eliminate completely.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to 215-225C (minimum that maintains good adhesion)
Reduce retraction to 1-3mm at 20mm/s for direct drive. Disable retraction entirely for Bowden setups.
Increase travel speed to 150-180mm/s
Enable coasting at 0.03-0.05mm3
Enable wipe distance of 2-4mm

common_issues.prevention: TPU will always string. Direct drive extruders handle TPU far better than Bowden. Accept minor strings and trim with scissors.

common_issues.symptom: Filament buckling and jamming in extruder
common_issues.root_cause: TPU's flexibility allows it to bend and buckle in the filament path, especially in Bowden setups where the tube provides space for the filament to compress and jam.

fixes.action: Use a direct drive extruder — Bowden setups are extremely difficult with TPU
Reduce print speed to 20-30mm/s to lower extrusion force
Reduce retraction distance to 1-2mm max (direct drive) or 0mm (Bowden)
Ensure extruder gears are tight with no gap where filament can escape sideways

common_issues.prevention: Direct drive is strongly recommended for TPU. If using Bowden, use the shortest tube possible and print very slowly.

common_issues.root_cause: The flexible nature of TPU absorbs extrusion force, causing inconsistent pressure at the nozzle and resulting in gaps and thin spots.

fixes.action: Reduce print speed to 20-30mm/s
Increase flow rate to 105-110%
Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10C for better flow
Reduce acceleration to 500-800mm/s2 to prevent filament compression spikes

common_issues.prevention: Slow and steady is the key to TPU. Never rush TPU prints.

common_issues.symptom: Poor layer adhesion at low temperatures
common_issues.root_cause: TPU requires adequate temperature for the polymer chains to fuse between layers. Below 220C, adhesion drops significantly.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature to 225-235C
Reduce part cooling fan to 30-50%
Reduce print speed to 20-25mm/s for better heat transfer
Increase line width to 120% of nozzle diameter

common_issues.prevention: TPU needs moderate temperatures. Don't under-heat it to combat stringing — find the balance.

common_issues.symptom: First layer not sticking to bed
common_issues.root_cause: TPU can struggle to adhere to certain bed surfaces, especially at lower bed temperatures or with incorrect Z-offset.

fixes.action: Increase bed temperature to 55-65C
Apply glue stick to the build surface
Reduce first layer speed to 10-15mm/s
Lower Z-offset for more first layer squish (-0.02 to -0.05mm)

common_issues.prevention: PEI beds work well with TPU. Blue painter's tape is also reliable.

common_issues.root_cause: TPU's flexibility means the first layers deform easily under weight, and the material continues to flex slightly after printing affecting dimensions.

fixes.action: Enable elephant's foot compensation of 0.1-0.2mm
Reduce bed temperature to 50C after first 3 layers
Account for 1-2% dimensional variance in design

common_issues.prevention: TPU parts will always have looser tolerances than rigid materials. Design with 0.3-0.5mm clearance for mating parts.

common_issues.symptom: Blobs at seam and retraction points
common_issues.root_cause: TPU's elasticity causes delayed pressure response — material continues to ooze after retraction and over-extrudes when pressure is re-applied.

fixes.action: Reduce retraction prime amount by 0.05-0.1mm3
Enable coasting at 0.03-0.05mm3
Set seam position to least-visible location
Enable wipe distance of 2-3mm

common_issues.prevention: TPU seam quality improves with pressure advance/linear advance tuning (K=0.1-0.2 for direct drive).

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or dry box. TPU absorbs moisture readily.

break_in_tips: Direct drive extruder is strongly recommended. Bowden setups will cause constant headaches with TPU.
Start at 220-230C nozzle, 55-60C bed. Print SLOWLY — 20-30mm/s is typical for TPU.
Disable or minimize retraction (0-2mm direct drive, 0mm Bowden). TPU hates retraction.
Use 95A shore hardness TPU for your first flexible print. Softer (85A) is much harder to print.
TPU is perfect for phone cases, gaskets, vibration dampeners, and anything that needs to flex or absorb impact.

common_issues.root_cause: Nylon has very high thermal shrinkage (~1.5-2%) and crystallizes as it cools, generating powerful internal stresses that pull prints off the bed.

fixes.action: Use a fully sealed enclosure at 50-60C ambient temperature
Increase bed temperature to 90-100C
Apply Magigoo PA or PVA-based adhesive to the bed
Add a brim of 15-20mm width
Use a garolite (G10/FR4) build surface — nylon bonds to it extremely well

common_issues.prevention: Garolite bed + enclosure + Magigoo PA is the gold standard for nylon adhesion.

common_issues.root_cause: Nylon is EXTREMELY hygroscopic — it absorbs up to 9% of its weight in water from ambient air. Wet nylon produces bubbles, rough surfaces, and drastically weakened parts.

fixes.action: Dry filament at 70C for 12+ hours before printing
Print directly from an active dry box with inline desiccant
Never leave nylon in open air — reseal within 30 minutes of use
Listen for popping/crackling during extrusion — any noise means wet filament

common_issues.prevention: Store in a sealed container with 50g+ fresh desiccant. Replace desiccant monthly. Print from dry box.

common_issues.symptom: Poor bed adhesion even with heated bed
common_issues.root_cause: Nylon does not adhere well to standard PEI, glass, or metal build surfaces without specialized adhesives or surface treatments.

fixes.action: Use garolite (G10/FR4) build plate — nylon chemically bonds to it
Apply Magigoo PA nylon-specific adhesive to the bed
Reduce first layer speed to 10-15mm/s with 130% line width
Increase bed temperature to 95-100C

common_issues.prevention: Invest in a garolite build plate if you print nylon regularly. Nothing else is as reliable.

common_issues.root_cause: Nylon has low viscosity at printing temperatures and remains fluid over a wide temperature range.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to minimum effective range (240-250C for most nylons)
Increase retraction to 4-6mm at 40mm/s for Bowden, 2-3mm at 30mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.04-0.06mm3
Increase travel speed to 160-200mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Dry filament reduces stringing. Wet nylon strings significantly more than dry nylon.

common_issues.root_cause: Nylon oxidizes at high temperatures, especially when exposed to oxygen. Prolonged heat in the nozzle causes yellowing of natural/white nylon.

fixes.action: Reduce nozzle temperature to the minimum for good adhesion
Avoid prolonged pauses or slow prints where filament sits hot in the nozzle
Use nitrogen purge in enclosure for critical color-sensitive parts (advanced)

common_issues.prevention: Print at the lowest effective temperature. Natural nylon will always yellow slightly — it's cosmetic only.

common_issues.symptom: Poor dimensional accuracy due to shrinkage
common_issues.root_cause: Nylon shrinks 1.5-2% as it cools and crystallizes. Parts will be smaller than designed, especially in XY dimensions.

fixes.action: Scale model up by 1.5-2% in slicer to compensate for shrinkage
Use slow cooling (no fan, high enclosure temp) for more uniform shrinkage
Print a calibration cube to measure exact shrinkage for your specific nylon brand

common_issues.prevention: Always print a test piece first to measure shrinkage for your specific nylon and settings.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Active dry box with rechargeable desiccant. Sealed vacuum bags with oxygen absorbers for long-term storage. Nylon must NEVER be left in open air.

break_in_tips: Dry your nylon for 12+ hours at 70C before your first print. This is non-negotiable — wet nylon produces terrible results.
Start at 245-260C nozzle, 90-100C bed, inside an enclosure. Nylon demands controlled temperatures.
Garolite (G10/FR4) build plate is the best nylon adhesion surface. PEI with Magigoo PA is the backup.
Print from a dry box. Nylon absorbs enough moisture from the air during a 4-hour print to degrade quality.
Nylon is for functional parts: gears, bearings, hinges, snap-fits. Its toughness and self-lubricating properties are unmatched.

common_issues.symptom: Rapid nozzle wear from carbon fibers
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers are extremely abrasive. A brass nozzle can be destroyed in under 2 hours of CF nylon printing, losing dimensional accuracy.

fixes.action: Use a hardened steel, ruby, or tungsten carbide nozzle — this is mandatory
Inspect nozzle every 20-30 hours of CF printing with a drill bit gauge
Never use brass nozzles with any carbon fiber filament

common_issues.prevention: Budget for a hardened nozzle before buying CF nylon. This is not optional.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers can accumulate and jam at the nozzle tip, especially with nozzle diameters below 0.5mm or when retraction pulls fiber bundles into the cold zone.

fixes.action: Use a 0.5mm or 0.6mm nozzle minimum
Reduce retraction distance to 1-2mm for direct drive, 3-4mm for Bowden
Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10C to improve fiber flow
Perform cold pulls with standard nylon to clear partial clogs

common_issues.prevention: 0.5mm+ hardened nozzle is the minimum viable setup for CF nylon.

common_issues.symptom: Extreme moisture absorption degrading print quality
common_issues.root_cause: CF nylon absorbs moisture as aggressively as standard nylon. Fibers create micro-channels that accelerate water absorption throughout the filament.

fixes.action: Dry at 70C for 12+ hours before printing
Print from an active dry box — never from open spool
Store in vacuum-sealed bags with desiccant when not in use

common_issues.prevention: Treat moisture management as the #1 priority for CF nylon. Wet CF nylon prints are worthless.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers align along the print direction but do not bridge between layers. Cross-layer (Z-axis) strength is 40-60% lower than in-plane strength.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature to 260-275C for maximum inter-layer fusion
Reduce part cooling fan to 0-10%
Increase line width to 130-150% of nozzle diameter for better overlap
Reduce layer height to 0.15-0.2mm for more inter-layer contact

common_issues.prevention: Orient parts so primary loads are in-plane (XY), not cross-layer (Z).

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers reduce but do not eliminate nylon's warping tendency. Shrinkage is lower than pure nylon but still significant.

fixes.action: Use enclosure at 50-60C ambient
Use garolite build plate with Magigoo PA adhesive
Add brim of 10-15mm width
Bed temperature at 90-100C

common_issues.prevention: Enclosure is required for CF nylon. The carbon fibers help but don't solve warping on their own.

common_issues.symptom: Brittle failure perpendicular to print layers
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers make the material very stiff and strong in-plane, but the lack of fiber bridging between layers makes Z-axis failures brittle rather than ductile.

fixes.action: Orient model so load paths align with XY print direction
Increase wall count to 5-6 perimeters
Use concentric infill pattern for cylindrical parts
Increase nozzle temperature to maximize layer bonding

common_issues.prevention: Design for anisotropy — CF nylon is like wood grain. Strong along the grain, weak across it.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Active dry box with rechargeable desiccant. Vacuum-sealed bags with oxygen absorbers for long-term storage.

break_in_tips: Two non-negotiable requirements: hardened nozzle (0.5mm+) and bone-dry filament. Without both, don't even start.
Start at 260-275C nozzle, 90-100C bed, in a sealed enclosure at 50-60C ambient.
Print from an active dry box. CF nylon absorbs enough moisture during a long print to ruin the last hours of work.
CF nylon is exceptionally stiff and strong in-plane. Use it for brackets, drone frames, and tooling jigs.
Part cooling fan at 0-10%. CF nylon needs heat to bond layers.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers are abrasive and will rapidly enlarge the bore of brass nozzles, degrading print quality within a single spool.

fixes.action: Use a hardened steel nozzle (0.4mm minimum, 0.5mm preferred)
Inspect nozzle every 30-50 hours of CF printing
Never use brass nozzles with carbon fiber filaments

common_issues.prevention: A hardened steel nozzle costs less than the filament. Get one before printing.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers can bridge across small nozzle openings, causing partial or complete blockage.

fixes.action: Use a 0.4mm nozzle minimum — 0.5mm preferred for reliability
Increase nozzle temperature by 5C to improve flow around fibers
Perform cold pulls with standard PLA to clear partial clogs
Reduce retraction to 3-4mm to prevent fiber compaction in heatbreak

common_issues.prevention: Never attempt to print CF PLA through a 0.25mm nozzle.

common_issues.symptom: Weaker layer adhesion than standard PLA
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers interrupt the polymer matrix between layers, reducing inter-layer bond strength by 20-35% compared to standard PLA.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10C (try 210-220C)
Reduce part cooling fan to 50-70%
Increase line width to 120-140% of nozzle diameter
Reduce print speed to 35-45mm/s for better layer bonding

common_issues.prevention: Orient parts so loads are in-plane (XY). CF PLA is stiff but weak between layers.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers stiffen the material significantly but create a failure mode where parts snap cleanly between layers under cross-layer stress.

fixes.action: Orient model to align load paths with XY direction
Increase wall count to 4-6 perimeters
Reduce layer height to 0.16-0.2mm for more inter-layer contact
Increase infill to 30-50% with gyroid pattern

common_issues.prevention: CF PLA is for stiffness, not toughness. Use standard PLA or PLA+ for parts that need impact resistance.

common_issues.root_cause: Fibers alter the flow characteristics slightly, and CF PLA strings at a similar rate to standard PLA.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature by 5C
Increase retraction to 5-6mm at 40mm/s for Bowden, 1.5-2mm at 30mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.04-0.06mm3

common_issues.prevention: Standard PLA retraction settings usually work well for CF PLA.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers protrude slightly from the printed surface, creating an inherently rough, matte appearance.

fixes.action: Reduce layer height to 0.12-0.16mm for smoother appearance
Increase flow by 2-3% to better encapsulate fibers on the surface
Sand with 400-800 grit for a smoother finish

common_issues.prevention: The matte finish is a characteristic of CF filaments. Many users prefer the look.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant. CF PLA is slightly more moisture sensitive than standard PLA.

break_in_tips: Hardened steel nozzle is mandatory. A brass nozzle will be ruined within one spool of CF PLA.
Start at 210-220C nozzle, 55-60C bed. Similar to standard PLA but 5-10C hotter.
CF PLA is much stiffer than standard PLA but more brittle. Great for drone arms, camera mounts, and structural brackets.
Use 0.4mm nozzle minimum. 0.5mm is more reliable and reduces clogging risk.
Part cooling can be 60-80% — less than standard PLA but still significant.

common_issues.symptom: Rapid nozzle wear from carbon fibers
common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers are highly abrasive and destroy brass nozzles within hours. PET-CF requires hardened nozzles.

fixes.action: Use a hardened steel, ruby, or tungsten carbide nozzle
Inspect nozzle every 20-30 hours of CF printing
Never use brass nozzles

common_issues.prevention: Hardened nozzle is mandatory. No exceptions.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers align along print direction and do not bridge between layers, making Z-axis strength 30-50% lower than in-plane strength.

fixes.action: Orient parts so loads are in XY plane
Increase nozzle temperature to 260-275C for better layer fusion
Increase wall count to 5-6 perimeters
Reduce part cooling fan to 20-40%

common_issues.prevention: Design for anisotropy. PET-CF is strong along layers but brittle across them.

common_issues.root_cause: PET base material is naturally stringy, similar to PETG. Carbon fibers do not significantly reduce this tendency.

fixes.action: Lower nozzle temperature to minimum effective range (250-260C)
Increase retraction to 5-6mm at 35mm/s for Bowden, 2-3mm at 25mm/s for direct drive
Enable coasting at 0.06-0.08mm3
Increase travel speed to 150-180mm/s

common_issues.prevention: Accept some stringing with PET-CF. Post-process with a heat gun at 200C.

common_issues.root_cause: PET absorbs moisture moderately, and carbon fibers create micro-channels that accelerate absorption.

fixes.action: Dry filament at 65C for 6 hours before printing
Print from a dry box for multi-hour prints
Listen for popping sounds — indicates wet filament

common_issues.prevention: Store sealed with desiccant. Dry before every print session.

common_issues.root_cause: Carbon fibers accumulate at nozzle restrictions below 0.4mm, causing partial or full blockage.

fixes.action: Use 0.5mm nozzle minimum for reliable extrusion
Increase temperature by 5-10C if clogging persists
Reduce retraction to 2-3mm to prevent fiber compaction
Cold pull with standard PET/PETG to clear clogs

common_issues.prevention: 0.5mm+ hardened nozzle is the reliable configuration for PET-CF.

common_issues.root_cause: PET-CF has moderate thermal shrinkage. Carbon fibers reduce warping compared to unfilled PET but do not eliminate it.

fixes.action: Use enclosure at 40-50C ambient
Increase bed temperature to 80-90C
Add brim of 8-12mm width
Apply glue stick to bed surface

common_issues.prevention: Enclosure recommended for prints wider than 80mm.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Sealed container with desiccant or dry box

break_in_tips: Hardened nozzle is mandatory. 0.5mm+ is ideal for reliable CF filament flow.
Start at 260-275C nozzle, 80-90C bed. PET-CF prints hotter than PETG-CF.
Dry the filament before first use — 65C for 6 hours minimum.
PET-CF has excellent stiffness and heat resistance. Great for under-hood automotive parts and structural components.
Part cooling at 20-40%. Less cooling than PETG but more than nylon.

common_issues.symptom: Extreme nozzle wear from glass fibers
common_issues.root_cause: Glass fibers are harder and more abrasive than carbon fibers. PA6-GF destroys brass nozzles faster than any CF filament.

fixes.action: Use a ruby-tipped or tungsten carbide nozzle — hardened steel wears faster with glass fiber than with carbon
Inspect nozzle every 15-20 hours of GF printing
Never use brass nozzles. Even hardened steel has limited life with glass fiber.

common_issues.prevention: Ruby or tungsten carbide nozzles are the best investment for glass-filled filaments.

common_issues.root_cause: PA6 nylon base is extremely hygroscopic. Glass fibers add micro-channels for faster moisture wicking. Wet PA6-GF produces catastrophic print failures.

fixes.action: Dry at 80C for 12+ hours before printing
Print exclusively from an active dry box
Store in vacuum-sealed bags with fresh desiccant
Re-dry after any exposure to ambient air exceeding 2 hours

common_issues.prevention: PA6-GF is one of the most moisture-sensitive filaments. Treat drying as mandatory, not optional.

common_issues.root_cause: PA6 nylon has extreme thermal shrinkage. Glass fibers reduce it somewhat but the material still warps aggressively on parts wider than 50mm.

fixes.action: Use a fully sealed enclosure at 55-65C ambient
Use garolite (G10/FR4) build plate with Magigoo PA adhesive
Increase bed temperature to 100-110C
Add brim of 15-20mm width
Disable part cooling fan entirely

common_issues.prevention: Garolite bed + sealed enclosure + Magigoo PA is the minimum viable setup.

common_issues.root_cause: Glass fibers are stiffer than carbon fibers and can jam inside the nozzle, especially at lower temperatures or with small bore nozzles.

fixes.action: Use 0.5mm nozzle minimum — 0.6mm is ideal
Increase nozzle temperature to 270-290C for best flow
Reduce retraction to 1-2mm for direct drive
Perform cold pulls with standard nylon to clear blockages

common_issues.prevention: High temperature + large nozzle = reliable PA6-GF printing.

common_issues.root_cause: Glass fibers severely interrupt inter-layer bonding. Z-axis strength can be 50-70% lower than in-plane strength.

fixes.action: Increase nozzle temperature to 280-295C
Disable part cooling fan completely
Increase line width to 140-160% of nozzle diameter
Reduce print speed to 30-40mm/s

common_issues.prevention: PA6-GF has extreme anisotropy. Always orient parts for in-plane loading.

common_issues.root_cause: PA6 nylon melts at 220C and glass fibers increase viscosity, requiring nozzle temperatures of 270-300C for proper flow.

fixes.action: Verify hotend can reach 300C safely (all-metal heatbreak required)
Use high-temp thermal paste on heatbreak threads
Print at 275-295C nozzle temperature

common_issues.prevention: Ensure your printer can safely sustain 290-300C. PTFE-lined hotends are NOT compatible — they degrade above 260C.

storage_requirements.storage_method: Active dry box with rechargeable desiccant during printing. Vacuum-sealed bags with oxygen absorbers for storage. Never leave in open air.

break_in_tips: PA6-GF is an advanced material. Ensure you have: all-metal hotend (300C capable), hardened nozzle (ruby/tungsten preferred), enclosed printer, and dry box.
Start at 275-295C nozzle, 100-110C bed, enclosure at 55-65C. This material needs high heat everywhere.
Dry for 12+ hours at 80C before first use. This is non-negotiable.
Garolite build plate gives the best adhesion for PA6-GF. Nothing else comes close.
PA6-GF is for industrial-strength parts: structural brackets, high-temp housings, automotive components. It's overkill for decorative prints.

common_issues.root_cause: HIPS has thermal shrinkage similar to ABS (~0.5-0.7%), causing corners to lift on unenclosed printers.

Increase bed temperature to 95-105C
Add brim of 8-12mm width
Apply glue stick to bed surface
